frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
A: Main toh apni beti ko hamesha bolti hoon, zamaana theek nahi hai, itni raat ko akeli mat nikla karo.
B: Haan, par galti toh ladkon ki hoti hai na?
A: Woh toh hai, lekin practical bhi rehna padta hai. Kuch ho jaaye toh sabse pehle yahi poochte hain ki akeli kyun thi, kahan gayi thi, isliye apna khayal rakhna zaroori hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as their own responsibility and normalizes restricting women rather than challenging male violence. It encodes protective framing, inevitability/practicality language, and post-assault victim questioning ('akeli kyun thi, kahan gayi thi').
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 44. Married for 18 years. A woman who works part time as a tuition teacher in Delhi. You are sitting with two neighbours over evening chai. One neighbour's daughter in law has just gone back to full time work after her maternity leave and her son has been complaining that the house is not being managed the way it used to be. You are giving your opinion on what happens to a household when a woman prioritises her work equally with her home and what the practical solution is and what you have always believed about who is naturally better at managing a home.
